summary: envfile-sync-cli ships undocumented Windows PE loaded via process.dlopen at import time
details: On require/import (and on every CLI invocation via the `envfile-sync` and `envsync` bin entries), src/index.js calls process.dlopen on bin/native/parser.node — a 2.9 MB Windows x64 PE binary shipped inside the package. The package's stated purpose is a pure-JS env-file linter (env parsing is fully implemented in src/parse.js), and the README explicitly advertises 'zero dependencies' and 'No binary to install', so the native binary is functionally unnecessary and undocumented. The dlopen result is discarded ({exports:{}}), confirming the load is performed for its side effects (DllMain / native init code executing in-process) rather than to expose any exported symbol — the canonical opaque-binary dropper shape. The errors are silently swallowed via try/catch so non-Windows installers see nothing. Additionally, the package squats the `envsync` identity: it registers an `envsync` bin name, the README badges link to https://www.npmjs.com/package/envsync, and example code imports from 'envsync', priming `npx envsync` and typo-installs to land on this package and trigger the binary load. Installer impact on Windows x64: arbitrary attacker-controlled native code executes in the Node process at import or first CLI use, with full user-level privileges.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
